channel.title: Virtual Gyans
channel.description:
Thanks for your precious time 🙂

Hyy Guys,
This is Irfan here !
Welcome to My Channel "Virtual Gyans".

Our Useful Contents:-✅

👉Educational Contents
      1. Interview Experiences.
      2. Company Review's.
      3. Comptetive Programming Questions.
      4. Placement  Questions and answers.
      5. CSE coding and core subject tutorials.
      6. Aptitude and Reasoning Tutorial's.
      6. Some contents for class 10.

👉Technical Contents
      1. Tech Tricks
      2. Tech Updates
      3. Gagets Reviews
      4. Gagets Unboxing
      5. Tips for Youtubers

👉For Businesses Enquiries:- virtualgyans@gmail.com ☑️

Thanks you for visiting  us ☄️

Jai Hind 🙏 Vande Mataram 🇮🇳

rounds.summary: Aptitude test containing logical reasoning, quantitative analysis, and verbal skills. Also included a SQL query MCQs section.
rounds.name: Round 1: Online Assessment / Aptitude Test

rounds.summary: Asked about OOPs features. Explain abstraction vs encapsulation. Write a small code implementing an interface in Java/C#.
rounds.name: Round 2: Technical Interview (TR)

rounds.summary: Asked: 'If you are assigned to a technology that you do not know, how will you handle it?'
rounds.name: Round 3: HR & Managerial Interview (MR/HR)

experiences.tips: Solve aptitude tests. Keep your resume concise. Show enthusiasm to learn new frameworks in the interview.

rounds.summary: InfyTQ Certification test. Extremely tough Python coding and database query questions. Focus on OOPs logic and data structures.
rounds.name: Round 1: Online Assessment / Aptitude Test

rounds.summary: Coding question: Find if two strings are anagrams of each other. I wrote an O(n) solution using hashmap.
rounds.name: Round 2: Technical Interview (TR)

rounds.summary: Asked about the company history and founders. Why Infosys? I mentioned their beautiful campuses and training center in Mysore.
rounds.name: Round 3: HR & Managerial Interview (MR/HR)

experiences.tips: Infosys InfyTQ is coding-heavy, so practice data structures thoroughly. Revise DBMS schemas and database indexes.

rounds.summary: Rapid fire MCQ on cognitive ability and basic IT topics. English section had reading comprehension and error spotting.
rounds.name: Round 1: Online Assessment / Aptitude Test

rounds.summary: Discussion on cloud computing. What is SaaS, PaaS, IaaS? What is the difference between SQL and NoSQL databases?
rounds.name: Round 2: Technical Interview (TR)

rounds.summary: Asked about my career plans for the next 3 to 5 years. I explained my goal of becoming a technical lead.
rounds.name: Round 3: HR & Managerial Interview (MR/HR)

experiences.tips: Communication assessment requires a quiet room and clear pronunciation. For the interview, talk about teamwork and collaboration.

rounds.summary: Cognitive assessment with rapid-fire questions on abstract reasoning, logic, and English. Technical section had pseudocode, MS Office, and cloud concepts.
rounds.name: Round 1: Online Assessment / Aptitude Test

rounds.summary: Discussion on Git commands. Explain the difference between git pull and git fetch. How do you resolve conflicts during git merge?
rounds.name: Round 2: Technical Interview (TR)

rounds.summary: Discussed my hobbies and extra-curricular activities. They asked how I handle pressure and work stress.
rounds.name: Round 3: HR & Managerial Interview (MR/HR)

experiences.tips: Practice solving pseudocodes on platforms like GeeksforGeeks. Ensure you write at least one coding question correctly to qualify.

rounds.summary: Online test with behavioral, English grammar, pseudo-code analysis, and basic mathematical sections.
rounds.name: Round 1: Online Assessment / Aptitude Test

rounds.summary: Asked to explain Java inheritance, interfaces, abstract classes. Write code demonstrating multiple inheritance using interfaces.
rounds.name: Round 2: Technical Interview (TR)

rounds.summary: Discussed my communication skills and how I ensure clarity when presenting projects to non-technical stakeholders.
rounds.name: Round 3: HR & Managerial Interview (MR/HR)

experiences.tips: Game-based aptitude tests are unique; watch tutorial videos on YouTube before attempting. Practice basic pseudo-codes.

rounds.summary: AMCAT-based assessment. Had sections on logical reasoning, quantitative ability, and debugging codes (Automata Fix).
rounds.name: Round 1: Online Assessment / Aptitude Test

rounds.summary: Asked about OOPs features. Explain abstraction vs encapsulation. Write a small code implementing an interface in Java/C#.
rounds.name: Round 2: Technical Interview (TR)

rounds.summary: Discussed my leadership qualities and instances where I took initiative in college events.
rounds.name: Round 3: HR & Managerial Interview (MR/HR)

experiences.tips: Rehearse your project details. Be prepared for SQL queries involving JOINS and aggregate functions. HR round is easy.

rounds.summary: Online test on TCS iON platform. Quant questions on time-work, speed-distance, probability. Coding questions: series summation and matrix transposing.
rounds.name: Round 1: Online Assessment / Aptitude Test

rounds.summary: Asked to write code to check if a string is a Palindrome. Also asked basic questions on pointers (C++) or memory management (Python).
rounds.name: Round 2: Technical Interview (TR)

rounds.summary: Asked why I want to join TCS. I talked about TCS being a global leader and its training programs like ILP.
rounds.name: Round 3: HR & Managerial Interview (MR/HR)

experiences.tips: Be strong in your final year project. Know the basic concepts of SDLC and DBMS. Relocation question should always be answered with 'Yes'.

rounds.summary: Online test with behavioral, English grammar, pseudo-code analysis, and basic mathematical sections.
rounds.name: Round 1: Online Assessment / Aptitude Test

rounds.summary: Asked to explain Java inheritance, interfaces, abstract classes. Write code demonstrating multiple inheritance using interfaces.
rounds.name: Round 2: Technical Interview (TR)

rounds.summary: Discussion on work-life balance and how I prioritize my tasks when multiple assignments are due.
rounds.name: Round 3: HR & Managerial Interview (MR/HR)

experiences.tips: Practice quantitative and reasoning MCQs. During the interview, show good soft skills and explain project challenges.

rounds.summary: Online test with quantitative, analytical, and verbal sections. Essay writing section on topics like 'Impact of AI on jobs'.
rounds.name: Round 1: Online Assessment / Aptitude Test

rounds.summary: Asked about Python data structures. Explain lists, dicts, and sets. When to use which? Write code to remove duplicates from a list.
rounds.name: Round 2: Technical Interview (TR)

rounds.summary: Asked about the Wipro service agreement (bond). Verified my willingness to sign it and relocate.
rounds.name: Round 3: HR & Managerial Interview (MR/HR)

experiences.tips: Revise basic data structures (Stacks, Queues, Linked Lists) and database normalization rules (1NF, 2NF, 3NF).

rounds.summary: Online test on Mettl. Verbal ability was moderate, quant had probability and permutation questions. Coding was on arrays.
rounds.name: Round 1: Online Assessment / Aptitude Test

rounds.summary: Asked about HTML, CSS, and JS basics. Explain CSS box model, difference between let and const in JS, and what is event bubbling.
rounds.name: Round 2: Technical Interview (TR)

rounds.summary: Asked about my salary expectations and willingness to work in shifts. Verified my educational documents.
rounds.name: Round 3: HR & Managerial Interview (MR/HR)

experiences.tips: Solve aptitude tests. Keep your resume concise. Show enthusiasm to learn new frameworks in the interview.

rounds.summary: HackwithInfy online coding challenge. Had 3 coding questions. I solved 1.5 questions to get selected for the interview.
rounds.name: Round 1: Online Assessment / Aptitude Test

rounds.summary: Coding question: Find if two strings are anagrams of each other. I wrote an O(n) solution using hashmap.
rounds.name: Round 2: Technical Interview (TR)

rounds.summary: Asked situational questions: 'If your manager gives you negative feedback, how will you react?'
rounds.name: Round 3: HR & Managerial Interview (MR/HR)

experiences.tips: Be strong in Operating System concepts (paging, virtual memory) and explain your coding logic clearly during the interview.

rounds.summary: Discussion on basic networking concepts. What is TCP/IP model? Explain the difference between HTTP and HTTPS protocols.

rounds.summary: Discussion on my family background and willingness to adapt to new corporate environments.
rounds.name: Round 3: HR & Managerial Interview (MR/HR)

flashcards.question: What is the difference between an Abstract Class and an Interface in Java?
flashcards.answer: An Abstract Class can have both abstract and concrete methods, can have state (instance variables), and supports single inheritance. An Interface can only have abstract methods (prior to Java 8), static/default methods (Java 8+), cannot have instance variables (only static final constants), and supports multiple inheritance.

flashcards.question: What is Method Overriding vs Method Overloading?
flashcards.answer: Overloading happens in the same class when two methods have the same name but different signatures (compile-time polymorphism). Overriding happens in child classes when a subclass redefines a parent class method with the identical signature (run-time polymorphism).

flashcards.question: How do you find the second highest salary in SQL?
flashcards.answer: Using subqueries: `SELECT MAX(Salary) FROM Employee WHERE Salary < (SELECT MAX(Salary) FROM Employee);` or using LIMIT/OFFSET: `SELECT Salary FROM Employee ORDER BY Salary DESC LIMIT 1 OFFSET 1;`.

flashcards.question: What is the difference between a Singly Linked List and a Doubly Linked List?
flashcards.answer: A Singly Linked List node contains data and a pointer to the next node (unidirectional traversal). A Doubly Linked List node contains data, a pointer to the next node, and a pointer to the previous node (bidirectional traversal, but consumes more memory per node).

flashcards.question: Explain the concept of Garbage Collection in Java.
flashcards.answer: Garbage Collection is an automatic process by the JVM that identifies and destroys objects that are no longer referenced in the heap memory, freeing up system resources. System.gc() can suggest execution, but doesn't guarantee immediate GC.

Final date and location of joining, including ILP center details.
Service Agreement uploading (usually requires stamp papers and guarantor signature) and medical certificate submissions.

content.text: 1. Introduction to C

C is a robust, procedural, middle-level programming language developed in 1972 by Dennis Ritchie at Bell Labs. It forms the foundation of modern languages and is widely used in systems programming, compiler design, and embedded platforms.
2. Memory Address & Pointer Basics
A pointer is a variable that stores the memory address of another variable. Understanding pointers is critical for dynamic memory management, array manipulation, and parameter passing in C.
3. Dynamic Memory Allocation

C provides functions in the <stdlib.h> header to allocate memory at runtime dynamically:
- malloc(size): Allocates specified bytes of uninitialized memory.
- calloc(num, size): Allocates memory and initializes all bits to zero.
- realloc(ptr, new_size): Resizes previously allocated memory block.
- free(ptr): Deallocates and releases memory back to the heap.
4. Structures vs Unions
Structures allocate a unique memory slot for each member, while Unions share a single memory slot among all members. The size of a union is equal to the size of its largest member.

notes.title: C Programming Quick Notes
notes.description: Essential syntax, pointer concepts, structures, dynamic memory allocation, and key interview snippets.

content.text: 1. Core Principles of OOPs

Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) in C++ uses four pillars:
1. Encapsulation: Bundling variables and functions inside a class while shielding them using access specifiers (private, protected, public).
2. Abstraction: Hiding implementation details and exposing minimal interfaces (using virtual/pure virtual functions).
3. Inheritance: Creating subclasses from a parent class to re-use code and extend properties.
4. Polymorphism: Allowing a call to act differently depending on context (e.g., function/operator overloading vs function overriding).
2. Runtime Polymorphism & Virtual Functions

Runtime polymorphism is achieved in C++ by declaring base class functions as virtual. It instructs the compiler to resolve function calls during runtime rather than compile-time based on the type of object referenced.
3. STL Containers Reference

C++ STL provides built-in data structures:
- Vector: Dynamic contiguous array. Fast inserts/deletions at the end.
- Map: Key-value lookup implemented as self-balancing Red-Black trees. Key operations are O(log n).
- Unordered Map: Key-value lookup using hash tables. Average O(1) time complexity.

notes.title: C++ & OOPs Revision Notes
notes.description: Object-oriented concepts, virtual functions, templates, STL (Standard Template Library), and C++ reference.

content.text: 1. Big-O Complexity Quick Guide

Big-O notation describes the upper bound scaling of execution time or memory footprint in the worst-case scenario:
- O(1): Constant time (Hash map lookup, array index).
- O(log n): Logarithmic time (Binary Search).
- O(n): Linear time (Linear search, single loop).
- O(n log n): Linearithmic time (Merge sort, Heap sort).
- O(n^2): Quadratic time (Bubble sort, nested loops).
2. Binary Search (Logarithmic Search)
Binary search finds elements inside a pre-sorted array by repeatedly splitting the search interval in half. Complexity is O(log n).
3. Graphs: BFS vs DFS

Breadth-First Search (BFS): Explores vertices level-by-level using a Queue. Ideal for finding the shortest path in unweighted graphs.
Depth-First Search (DFS): Explores nodes as deep as possible before backtracking using a Stack or recursion. Ideal for topology sorting and path checking.

notes.title: DSA Essential Hand Book
notes.description: Overview of major data structures, sorting/searching algorithms, complexity analysis, and coding patterns.

content.text: Q1: Explain ACID Properties in DBMS

Transactions must support ACID rules:
- Atomicity: Full transaction success or complete rollback.
- Consistency: Transition database from one valid integrity state to another.
- Isolation: Transactions run concurrently without interfering.
- Durability: Committed transactions survive crashes (saved permanently).
Q2: How does a HashMap work internally in Java?

HashMap stores key-value pairs in buckets based on the key's hashcode. It maps indices using (n - 1) & hash. If multiple keys hash to the same index (collision), they are chained in a LinkedList. In Java 8, if a LinkedList size in a bucket exceeds 8 and the table capacity is ≥ 64, it converts the list into a Red-Black Tree for faster O(log n) lookups.
Q3: Explain differences between REST and SOAP

- REST: Architectural style, uses HTTP methods, lighter payload formats (JSON, XML, HTML), stateless, easily scalable.
- SOAP: Strict protocol, uses XML only, requires WSDL contracts, supports built-in WS-Security, heavier payload, supports stateful transactions.
Q4: Describe a time you faced team conflicts (HR STAR Method)

Situation: In our final year project, we had to choose between SQL and MongoDB. Two members clashed heavily.
Task: As the project coordinator, I had to resolve the conflict without affecting the timeline.
Action: I scheduled a meeting, listened to both ideas, listed the pros and cons of SQL vs NoSQL relative to our specific requirements, and proposed a vote based on technical fit.
Result: We chose SQL, completed the project 1 week early, and maintained great team relationships.

notes.title: Top 50 Technical Interview Questions
notes.description: Hand-picked high-frequency technical and behavioral interview questions with standard answers.

content.text: 1. JVM Execution Lifecycle & Memory Zones

JVM executes Java bytecode. The memory layout is partitioned into five distinct areas:
1. Heap Area: Stores all object instances and arrays. Managed by Garbage Collection.
2. Stack Area: Stores local variables and execution frames for individual threads.
3. Method Area: Stores class metadata, bytecode, static variables, and runtime constant pools.
4. PC Registers: Contains the address of current instruction being executed.
5. Native Method Stack: Holds instructions for native libraries (C/C++).
2. Java Collections Core Hierarchy

- List: ArrayList (resizing array, O(1) random read) vs LinkedList (doubly linked list, O(1) insertions).
- Set: HashSet (hash table lookup, unordered) vs TreeSet (Red-Black tree, sorted, unique).
- Map: HashMap (hash-bucket array with tree conversion when list threshold > 8) vs TreeMap (sorted keys).
3. Java 8 Streams and Functional Coding

notes.title: Java Programming Master Notes
notes.description: Core Java, JVM architecture, multithreading, collections framework, Java 8 features (lambdas/streams).

content.text: 1. Key Microservices Patterns

Microservices break a large monolithic app into small, autonomous, deployable services, requiring specialized patterns:
- Service Discovery (Eureka): Registries mapping all microservice endpoints so they can search for each other.
- API Gateway (Spring Cloud Gateway): Central proxy redirecting user requests, handling JWT validation and routing rules.
- Circuit Breaker (Resilience4j): Prevents cascading network crashes by failing fast once a targeted downstream service fails threshold requirements.
2. Async Communication via Kafka
To prevent blocking threads, microservices use message queues like Apache Kafka or RabbitMQ. This permits highly decoupled, event-driven integrations.

notes.title: Microservices Architecture Blueprints
notes.description: Discovery services (Eureka), API Gateway, Circuit Breaker patterns, configuration server, and Kafka integration.

content.text: 1. Pythonic Collections and Complexities

Python has key built-in data types:
- List: Ordered, mutable dynamic array. O(1) index lookup.
- Tuple: Ordered, immutable, hashable sequence.
- Set: Unordered, mutable collection of unique elements. O(1) lookups.
- Dictionary: Hash-table based mapping of key-value pairs. O(1) average lookups.
2. Decorators and Metaprogramming
Decorators wrap another function to extend its behavior dynamically without altering the original function source code.
3. Generators & Memory Efficiency
Generators evaluate expressions lazily, returning an iterator that yields one item at a time. This saves substantial memory when working with large data ranges.

notes.title: Python Cheat Sheet & Reference
notes.description: Core concepts, list comprehensions, decorators, generators, OOPs in Python, and common libraries.

content.text: 1. Spring IoC & Dependency Injection

Spring inversion of Control (IoC) Container manages bean creation, lifecycle, and mapping. Dependency Injection (DI) allows dependencies to be injected dynamically instead of hardcoding object instantiation inside controllers/services.
2. Core Annotations Cheat Sheet

- @SpringBootApplication: Combines Configuration, EnableAutoConfiguration, and ComponentScan.
- @RestController: Marks a class as a controller returning REST JSON payloads.
- @Autowired: Marks a dependency to be injected automatically by Spring.
- @Service / @Repository / @Component: Marks classes as Spring Beans with specific architectural roles.
3. Rest API Controller Example

notes.title: Spring Boot Core Concepts
notes.description: Spring framework core, IoC, Dependency Injection, REST APIs, annotations reference, and Spring Data JPA.

content.text: 1. SQL Commands Classification

- DDL (Data Definition): CREATE, ALTER, DROP, TRUNCATE (defines database schemas).
- DML (Data Manipulation): SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE (manages records).
- DCL (Data Control): GRANT, REVOKE (controls roles and privileges).
- TCL (Transaction Control): COMMIT, ROLLBACK, SAVEPOINT.
2. Database Joins
Joins link tables based on shared column relationships. A LEFT JOIN fetches everything from the left table and matches from the right table, leaving NULLs for unmatched keys.

-- Fetch employees and their department details
SELECT e.id, e.name, d.department_name
FROM employees e
INNER JOIN departments d ON e.dept_id = d.id;
3. Window Functions & Aggregations
Window functions evaluate partitions without collapsing table records into unified groups. DENSE_RANK() returns ranking rows with no gaps in values.

-- Rank employee salaries in their departments
SELECT name, dept_id, salary,
       DENSE_RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY dept_id ORDER BY salary DESC) as rank
FROM employees;

notes.title: SQL & Relational Databases Quick Guide
notes.description: SQL DDL/DML, Joins, grouping and aggregations, window functions, indexing strategies, and ACID properties.

content.text: 1. Scalability: Scale Up vs Scale Out

Vertical Scaling (Scale Up): Adding resources (RAM, CPU) to a single machine. Hard limits apply and creates a Single Point of Failure (SPOF).
Horizontal Scaling (Scale Out): Adding more servers to the cluster. Indefinite scalability, but requires load balancers and complex data sync.
2. Load Balancers
Load Balancers distribute user traffic across servers to optimize availability. Common algorithms include Round Robin, Weighted Round Robin, Least Connections, and IP Hashing.
3. Caching & Redis

Caching stores high-frequency data in memory (Redis, Memcached) to reduce database read pressure. Common patterns include:
- Cache-Aside: Application checks cache; if miss, fetches from database and writes back to cache.
- Write-Through: Application writes to cache, cache writes to database synchronously.
- Write-Behind (Write-Back): Application writes to cache, database is updated asynchronously (fastest, but risk of data loss).
4. Databases: SQL vs NoSQL

- SQL (Relational): Schema-enforced, structured tables, supports ACID transactions. Scaled vertically. Ideal for payments and billing.
- NoSQL (Non-Relational): Flexible schema, Key-Value (Redis), Document (MongoDB), Column (Cassandra), Graph (Neo4j). Scaled horizontally. Ideal for social feeds, chat logs.

notes.title: System Design Fundamentals
notes.description: High-level architecture, scalability, load balancers, caching, databases (SQL vs NoSQL), and CAP theorem.

playground_questions.title: 4. Find Peak Element

solutions.python:
def find_peak_element(nums, target):
    l, r = 0, len(nums) - 1
    while l <= r:
        mid = (l + r) // 2
        if nums[mid] == target: return mid
        elif nums[mid] < target: l = mid + 1
        else: r = mid - 1
    return -1

playground_questions.title: 6. Product of Array Except Self

solutions.python:
def product_of_array_except_self(nums):
    n = len(nums)
    res = [1] * n
    prefix = 1
    for i in range(n):
        res[i] = prefix
        prefix *= nums[i]
    postfix = 1
    for i in range(n - 1, -1, -1):
        res[i] *= postfix
        postfix *= nums[i]
    return res

playground_questions.title: 15. Search in Rotated Array

solutions.python:
def search_in_rotated_array(nums, target):
    l, r = 0, len(nums) - 1
    while l <= r:
        mid = (l + r) // 2
        if nums[mid] == target: return mid
        elif nums[mid] < target: l = mid + 1
        else: r = mid - 1
    return -1

playground_questions.title: 46. Is Subsequence

solutions.python:
def is_subsequence(nums):
    if not nums: return 0
    rob1 = rob2 = 0
    for n in nums:
        temp = max(n + rob1, rob2)
        rob1 = rob2
        rob2 = temp
    return rob2

playground_questions.title: 177. Word Search

solutions.python:
def word_search(nums, target):
    l, r = 0, len(nums) - 1
    while l <= r:
        mid = (l + r) // 2
        if nums[mid] == target: return mid
        elif nums[mid] < target: l = mid + 1
        else: r = mid - 1
    return -1

playground_questions.title: 193. House Robber

solutions.python:
def house_robber(nums):
    if not nums: return 0
    rob1 = rob2 = 0
    for n in nums:
        temp = max(n + rob1, rob2)
        rob1 = rob2
        rob2 = temp
    return rob2

playground_questions.title: 215. Target Sum DP

solutions.python:
def target_sum_dp(nums):
    if not nums: return 0
    rob1 = rob2 = 0
    for n in nums:
        temp = max(n + rob1, rob2)
        rob1 = rob2
        rob2 = temp
    return rob2

playground_questions.title: 226. Binary Search

solutions.python:
def binary_search(nums, target):
    l, r = 0, len(nums) - 1
    while l <= r:
        mid = (l + r) // 2
        if nums[mid] == target: return mid
        elif nums[mid] < target: l = mid + 1
        else: r = mid - 1
    return -1

playground_questions.title: 241. Search Rotated Array II

solutions.python:
def search_rotated_array_ii(nums, target):
    l, r = 0, len(nums) - 1
    while l <= r:
        mid = (l + r) // 2
        if nums[mid] == target: return mid
        elif nums[mid] < target: l = mid + 1
        else: r = mid - 1
    return -1

solutions.postgres:
SELECT d.department_name, COUNT(e.id) AS total_employees, AVG(e.salary) AS avg_salary
FROM departments d
LEFT JOIN employees e ON d.id = e.department_id
GROUP BY d.department_name;

GROUP BY d.department_name;

SELECT d.department_name, COUNT(e.id) AS total_employees, AVG(e.salary) AS avg_salary

GROUP BY d.department_name;
